schools.summary: 'Great conflict' (helikon agona, an inward contest of anxiety and prayer, the same 'striving' as Phil 1:30) for Colosse, Laodicea, and all 'who have not seen my face' — churches Paul never visited, loved as his own; he opens with affection to win their trust (Calvin).

The threefold aim — hearts 'comforted' (encouraged, strengthened), 'knit together in love' (synbibasthentes, welded into one body, Eph 4:16), and brought to 'all riches of the full assurance of understanding'; the climax 'the mystery of God... even Christ' — Christ himself the once-hidden mystery, the sum of all true knowledge, against the false teachers' speculations.

themes: the mystery of God, even Christ

schools.summary: All wisdom concentrated in Christ — 'all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' laid up in him as a storehouse, 'hid' (stored, to be found in him and nowhere else, not concealed from believers); Ellicott notes 'hidden' was the false teachers' word for secret lore, turned against them — the only true hidden treasure is Christ.

themes: all treasures of wisdom hid in Christ

schools.summary: The protective purpose — 'beguile' (paralogizetai, delude by false reasoning, cheat by sophistry resting on false premises) with 'enticing words' (persuasive, plausible speech); the all-sufficiency of Christ (vv2-3) the antidote, the conviction that closes the door against all errors (Calvin).

'Absent in the flesh' yet 'with you in the spirit' (his concern making him as present); military imagery — 'your order' (taxin, unbroken rank, disciplined fellowship) and 'stedfastness' (stereoma, a solid front that holds); a few stragglers but no general defection; praise as a spur to perseverance (Calvin).

themes: absent in flesh, present in spirit

schools.summary: The governing rule — 'as ye received Christ Jesus the Lord' (in his fullness, Saviour and Sovereign) 'so walk in him'; continue as you began, for Paul brings 'no strange addition' unlike the false teachers (Chrysostom); to have received Christ profits nothing unless one remains in him (Calvin) — perseverance, not mere beginning.

Mixed metaphors of the walk in Christ — 'rooted' (errizomenoi, perfect, rooted once for all and staying, drawing all from him), 'built up' (present, rising continually on the foundation, Eph 3:17), 'stablished in the faith as ye were taught,' 'abounding with thanksgiving'; the settled and the growing combined — depth, firmness, growth, gratitude.

themes: rooted and built up in him

schools.summary: 'Spoil' (sylagogon, carry off as plunder, kidnap and enslave); not philosophy as such (true study confirms faith, Henry) but 'philosophy and vain deceit' — empty speculation blended with Christianity; its threefold mark: 'after the tradition of men' (human invention), 'after the rudiments of the world' (elementary worldly principles), 'not after Christ' (the decisive test).

themes: tradition of men, rudiments of the world

schools.summary: The heart of the message — 'all the fulness' (entire completeness) 'of the Godhead' (theotetos, deity itself, the divine nature, not mere influence) 'dwelleth' (katoikei, abides permanently) in Christ 'bodily' (somatikos, in his incarnate person); since the entire fullness of God resides in him, no room for supplements or mediators.

The believer 'complete' (pepleromenoi, filled full, lacking nothing) in Christ — the philosophy empty (kene), Christ the fullness (pleroma), the once-empty now filled in him (John 1:16; Eph 3:19); a derived but entire perfection; Christ 'the head of all principality and power,' supreme over the very angels the false teachers would worship.

The Judaizing demand answered by the inward reality — 'circumcision made without hands' (acheiropoietos, a spiritual operation, circumcision of the heart, Rom 2:29), 'putting off the body of the sins of the flesh' (the whole sinful nature, not a little skin), 'the circumcision of Christ' (which he performs by his Spirit); Mosaic circumcision needless, the substance having come (Calvin).

themes: putting off the body of sin

schools.summary: Union with Christ in baptism — 'buried with him' (syntaphentes, Rom 6:4, the old life laid in the grave) and 'risen with him' to new life; the allusion to immersion (plunging under and emerging) though no stress on the mode (Wesley); effected 'through the faith of the operation of God' (not the water but faith in God's mighty working); baptism displacing circumcision as the covenant sign (Calvin).

themes: faith of the operation of God
death to sin and new life

schools.summary: Applied to the Gentile believers — 'dead in your sins' (moral death, separated from God, Eph 2:1) 'and the uncircumcision of your flesh' (their Gentile state and corrupt nature, doubly dead, Wesley); 'quickened together with him' (made alive with Christ's resurrection life); 'having forgiven all trespasses' (a full free pardon, for there is no life without forgiveness).

The law's condemning bond cancelled — a 'handwriting' (a signed certificate of debt) 'of ordinances' (the law, esp. ceremonial) 'against us... contrary to us' (a hostile witness exacting the penalty); God 'blotted it out' (exaleipsas, wiped clean), 'took it out of the way,' 'nailing it to his cross' (cancelled in Christ's crucified body); the ceremonies the very bond holding men guilty, now abolished (Calvin).

Christ's triumph over the powers — 'spoiled' the principalities (stripped the hostile spiritual rulers/devils of their arms), disarming them so they can no longer accuse us (the bond destroyed, v14, Calvin); 'made a shew of them openly' (captives in a victory procession), 'triumphing in it' (the cross, the instrument of apparent defeat, the scene of decisive victory).
Cruxes (Robertson): whether the subject is God or Christ (the Father acting in the Son), and whether apekdysamenos means 'put off from himself' the powers or 'stripped' them.

'Therefore' (because complete in Christ, the bond blotted out) 'let no man judge you' (krineto) — over 'meat and drink' (clean/unclean foods) or sacred times ('holyday' yearly festivals, 'new moon' monthly, 'sabbath' weekly); the ceremonial observances now fulfilled and abolished in Christ, their enforcement without authority; against the Judaizing wing of the Colossian error.

The ceremonies 'a shadow' (skia, a dim outline cast ahead by the coming reality) of things to come, 'but the body' (soma, the substance casting the shadow) 'is of Christ' (Heb 10:1); a shadow is valued while the substance is absent, but to cling to it once the body has come is absurd, like judging a man by his shadow while he stands before you (Calvin).

themes: a shadow of things to come

schools.summary: The athletic figure — 'beguile you of your reward' (katabrabeueto, an unjust umpire robbing the runner of his prize, the heavenly reward, Phil 3:14); the error's twofold mask — 'voluntary humility' (affected lowliness pretending men unworthy to approach God) and 'worshipping of angels' (angelic mediators instead of Christ); really pride (Henry, Wesley), 'intruding into things not seen,' 'puffed up by a fleshly mind.'

themes: puffed up by a fleshly mind

schools.summary: The fatal flaw — 'not holding the Head' (ou kraton, actually deserting Christ, dethroning him for angels/mediators); one does not hold Christ who trusts not in him alone (Wesley); the body figure — from the Head 'by joints and bands' the body receives nourishment, is knit together, and 'increaseth with the increase of God'; all life and growth flow from Christ alone (Calvin), severed from whom the body withers.

The argument from union — 'if ye died with Christ' (assumed true, the burial in baptism, 2:12) to 'the rudiments of the world' (the elementary ceremonial principles, 2:8), why 'as though living in the world' submit to 'ordinances'? It is inconsistent for the dead to be bound by the rules of the life they left; we are dead to the law and the law to us (Calvin, Gal 2:19).

themes: from the rudiments of the world

schools.summary: Paul quotes the ascetics' own rules (not his commands) — 'specimens of Gnostic rules,' a string of negatives; Ellicott notes a climax of strictness in the original order ('handle not, taste not, touch not' with a finger) over food and ritual purity; all prohibitions and abstentions, a religion of 'thou shalt not' about external, perishable things.

themes: touch not, taste not, handle not

schools.summary: A double refutation — the forbidden things 'perish with the using' (perishable, decaying in the consuming, belonging to the body, with no bearing on God's spiritual kingdom), and the rules are 'after the commandments and doctrines of men' (mere human invention, lacking God's authority, as Christ charged the Pharisees' traditions); their object perishable, their source merely human (Calvin).

A mere appearance conceded — 'a shew of wisdom' (a plausible repute of piety) in 'will worship' (self-imposed religion God has not commanded), 'humility' (affected lowliness), and 'neglecting of the body' (harsh asceticism); but 'not in any honour' (of no value before God) — the severe self-denial not truly checking the flesh but feeding pride and self-will (Wesley); the show is all there is.
